My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the series has clearly broken above the 2.24-2.36 band I set as my flat threshold, sustained over multiple releases, and is now running +0.39pp above the 2% target. raw_response: {"report": "The last print (2.36) confirms the modest reversal I flagged as a risk: breakeven_10y broke below the 2.38 level that had held for months, falling from 2.38\u21922.35\u21922.36 in the last three readings. Given the recent downtick is real but small and unconfirmed by a break of the 12-month low, I lean modestly toward continued softening rather than a rebound, but without strong conviction since the move is only a few basis points and could easily mean-revert back toward 2.38.", "key_evidence": "breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target", "falsifier": "A move back above 2.38 sustained for two releases would negate the softening; a break below 2.33 would confirm a genuine downtrend.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "inflation", "why": "Realized CPI surprises often lead breakeven moves and would clarify if softening is fundamentals-driven."}, {"driver": "term_premium", "why": "Breakeven moves can reflect liquidity/term premium shifts rather than true inflation expectation changes."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The last print (2.36) confirms the modest reversal I flagged as a risk: breakeven_10y broke below the 2.38 level that had held for months, falling from 2.38→2.35→2.36 in the last three readings. The 21-day change is now -0.05pp, having flipped negative after a longer uptrend (63d +0.07, 252d +0.18), suggesting the multi-quarter rise in inflation expectations is stalling or mildly reversing at the margin. That said, the series remains range-bound at a very tight level (2.35-2.39 over ten prints), and the drop is small — one or two bp moves, not a decisive break. Range position (0.68) still shows breakevens closer to the top of their 12-month range (2.15-2.46), and the level remains 0.36pp above the 2% target, consistent with inflation expectations still anchored moderately above objective rather than collapsing. My prior flat call was roughly right in the sense that the level barely moved, but I underweighted the emerging short-term softening. Given the recent downtick is real but small and unconfirmed by a break of the 12-month low, I lean modestly toward continued softening rather than a rebound, but without strong conviction since the move is only a few basis points and could easily mean-revert back toward 2.38.

view.report: The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by the data. Breakeven has drifted down from 2.28 to 2.23 over the shown window, with be_change_21d -0.05pp and be_change_63d -0.20pp confirming a steady, not noisy, decline. The last four readings (2.24, 2.23, 2.22, 2.23) show the decline decelerating and possibly stabilizing near 2.22-2.23, but there's no sign of a bounce back toward the 2.28 starting point. be_range_position of 0.21 shows breakeven sitting in the lower fifth of its 12-month range, and it's now only 0.06pp above the 12-month low, having fallen from a high of 2.46. Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.23pp, so expectations are still anchored above target, but the trend of the last one-to-three months is clearly downward. Given the my prior threshold (sustained move below 2.26 for two consecutive releases), that condition is now met — the last four prints are all below 2.26. This warrants revising to a "down" lean, though conviction is moderate since the last two prints (2.22, 2.23) suggest the pace of decline is flattening out near current levels rather than accelerating further.
view.falsifier: A move back above 2.26 sustained for two consecutive releases, or renewed upward acceleration in be_change_21d, would invalidate the down call.

missing_inputs.why: Realized CPI surprises often drive short-term breakeven moves and would clarify if decline reflects falling inflation risk premium.
Breakeven moves can reflect term premium/liquidity effects rather than pure inflation expectations shifts.

user_prompt:
Driver: inflation_expectations

breakeven_10y (%) — last 10 observations, oldest → newest
  2.24, 2.24, 2.24, 2.23, 2.24, 2.24, 2.23, 2.22, 2.24, 2.25

Derived measurements
  be_change_21d            +0.01 pp
  be_change_63d            -0.10 pp
  be_change_252d           -0.10 pp
  be_distance_from_target  +0.25 pp
  be_12m_low               +2.17 %
  be_12m_high              +2.46 %
  be_above_12m_low         +0.08 pp
  be_range_width           +0.29 pp
  be_range_position        +0.28 0-1

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Available indicators suggest that economic activity has been expanding at a moderate pace.
  · Job gains have slowed this year, and the unemployment rate has edged up through September.
  · More recent indicators are consistent with these developments.
  · Inflation has moved up since earlier in the year and remains somewhat elevated.
  · The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run.
  · Uncertainty about the economic outlook remains elevated.
  · The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate and judges that downside risks to employment rose in recent months.
  · In support of its goals and in light of the shift in the balance of risks, the Committee decided to lower the target range for the federal funds rate by 1/4 percentage point to 3-1/2 to 3‑3/4 percent.
  · In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks.
  · The Committee is strongly committed to supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.
  · In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.
  · The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals.
  · The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
  · The Committee judges that reserve balances have declined to ample levels and will initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasury securities as needed to maintain an ample supply of reserves on an ongoing basis.
  · Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H.
  · Powell, Chair; John C.
  · Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S.
  · Barr; Michelle W.
  · Bowman; Susan M.
  · Collins; Lisa D.
  · Cook; Philip N.
  · Jefferson; Alberto G.
  · Musalem; and Christopher J.
  · Waller.
  · Voting against this action were Stephen I.
  · Miran, who preferred to lower the target range for the federal funds rate by 1/2 percentage point at this meeting; and Austan D.
  · Goolsbee and Jeffrey R.
  · Schmid, who preferred no change to the target range for the federal funds rate at this meeting.

Your previous view
  At the previous release you called this driver down, with conviction 0.45.
  You said you would change your mind if: A move back above 2.26 sustained for two consecutive releases, or renewed upward acceleration in be_change_21d, would invalidate the down call.

view.reasoning: The last print (2.25) ticked up from 2.22-2.24 range, and be_change_21d is now +0.01pp — a mild reversal from the prior modest downtrend. However, be_change_63d and be_change_252d remain firmly negative at -0.10pp each, showing the medium/longer-term trend is still down from recent highs. My previous "down" call at 0.45 conviction is only partially validated: the multi-month decline did occur (confirmed by the -0.10pp readings), but the most recent single observation shows a small bounce, not continuation. Breakevens sit at 2.25%, still 0.25pp above the 2% target, in the upper-middle of the 12-month range (0.28 range position, closer to low than high). The series is essentially flat over the last 10 observations (2.22-2.25 band), with no clear acceleration in either direction. Policy language is unchanged/boilerplate on inflation expectations, offering no new signal. Given the tiny recent uptick against a backdrop of a stalled-but-not-reversing longer-term decline, I see this as a wash — the data doesn't support continuing the down call with the same conviction, nor does it support flipping to up. This looks like consolidation near 2.23-2.25 rather than fresh directional momentum.
